senses.gloss: to be bent, to be tilted, to be slanted
verbal noun of مَالَ (māla, “to lean”) (form I)
reach of the eye, as far as one can see
a kind of metal probe in form of a needle for applying collyrium or other treatments
plural of أَمْيَل (ʔamyal, “leaning to one side”)

etymology.text:
From م ي ل (m y l), meaning to incline, to bend, to lean.

From Latin mīlle passuum (literally “a thousand of paces”); compare Classical Syriac ܡܝܠܐ (mīlā) and Hebrew מַיְל (mayl).
The reach of an eye originates from a folk etymology explaining the distance of a mile, literally the distance to which the eye reaches along land before the horizon curves out of sight.

From Ancient Greek μήλη (mḗlē, “probe”).

See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

body.text: distance to which the eye reaches along land:

but the difference is merely verbal; for they agree that its extent is ninety-six thousand digits; [about 5166 English feet;] each digit being the measure of six barley-corns, each placed with its belly next to another; but the ancients say that the cubit is thirty-two digits; which makes the mile three thousand cubits. (Msb, which see for more.) See also
